sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Functional capacity assessment estimates whether a patient can safely perform essential work demands. It should combine symptoms, observed function, risk, and the actual job task rather than relying on pain scores alone.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Reported difficulty with lifting, climbing, kneeling, walking, driving, or repetitive tasks
- Concern about whether duties can be performed safely
- Recovering worker needing graded return planning
- Mismatch between reported symptoms and required physical tasks

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Fit-for-work decisions should consider safety-critical tasks, endurance, cognition, medications, and environment - not just local symptoms
- Severe pain, poor sleep, opioid use, dizziness, or neurological deficit may affect work safety even if the injury itself seems minor
- Permanent restrictions should not be made from a single short review without adequate information

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Clarify the essential duties, current symptoms, and any immediate safety risks
- Screen whether the worker can safely mobilise, drive, climb, lift, or use tools today
- Escalate to medical review if the worker appears unsafe, medically unstable, or significantly impaired

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Match observed function to actual job demands, not generic labels such as 'light duties'
- Use functional testing only when it is safe, relevant, and unlikely to aggravate the condition significantly
- Recommend clear restrictions, tolerances, and review points for graded return rather than open-ended work exclusion
- Coordinate with medical, supervisory, and rehabilitation stakeholders where appropriate

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Useful restrictions are specific, time limited, and linked to tasks such as lifting, climbing, kneeling, driving, or shift duration
